message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent of all others. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correctly addresses the issue, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it, with a discriminating regression test where a code change is involved).

Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix F-013 (fix) in .github/workflows/ci.yml
What was done: license gate scans the hash-pinned lockfile, not pip install -e .[all].
Regression test(s): test_license_gate_scans_pinned_lockfile_not_live_resolution

Steps:
1. Read the actual changed file(s) at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
2. Read the named regression test(s) and judge whether they actually discriminate the fix (would fail if the fix were reverted).
3. For a 'reclassified-false-positive', 'scoped-decline', or 'deferred-by-mandate' item: judge whether that JUDGMENT is correct and well-justified, not whether code changed.
4. Decide: appropriate? complete? Overall verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested).

Be skeptical and specific. Return your verdict.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

120	          IGNORES=()
121	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
122	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
123	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
124	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
125	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
126	          fi
127	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
128	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
129	        run: |
130	          set -euo pipefail
131	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
132	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
133	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
134	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
135	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json
136	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
137	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
138	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
139	        if: always()
140	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4

142	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
143	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
144	          if-no-files-found: error
145	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
146	        run: |
147	          set -euo pipefail
148	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
149	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
150	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
151	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.

154	          python -m pip install pip-licenses

content.text:
The fix is confirmed. Let me verify the test would actually fail on revert by checking the buggy version contained `pip install -e ".[all]"`.

The old version (1e3dce5) had `pip install -e ".[all]"` and no `--require-hashes -r requirements.lock` in the license gate section. The test:
- `assert 'pip install -e ".[all]"' not in gate_section` — would fail on revert (old code had exactly this string)
- `assert "--require-hashes -r requirements.lock" in gate_section` — would fail on revert (old code lacked it)

Both assertions discriminate. Let me confirm `_ci_text` and the gate_section slicing are sound, and run the test.

content.text:
Tree is clean. `requirements.lock` is comprehensive (4692 lines), and the comment correctly explains the lock is generated from the full extras set, so it covers what `.[all]` would. The `--no-deps -e .` for the local package is correct (avoids re-resolving deps outside the pinned set). pip-licenses then scans the installed (pinned) environment.

Assessment:
- Appropriate: The change replaces the live `pip install -e ".[all]"` resolution with `pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock` + `--no-deps -e .`, so the license gate scans exactly the hash-pinned set that pip-audit/SBOM audited. This is idiomatic, consistent with sibling CI steps (core/audit use the same pattern), no regression. The accompanying comment cites #F-013 and explains the rationale.
- Complete: The named regression test discriminates — it asserts both the absence of `.[all]` and the presence of `--require-hashes -r requirements.lock` within the license-gate section. Empirically verified to fail on a scoped revert of just this fix and pass on the fixed tree.

input.reasoning:
Appropriate: In /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml the "License gate" step (lines 145-156) was changed from a live `python -m pip install --upgrade pip-licenses; pip install -e ".[all]"` resolution to `pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock` + `pip install --no-deps -e .`, then pip-licenses + scripts/license_gate.py. This makes the license gate scan exactly the hash-pinned dependency set that pip-audit (`-r requirements.lock`, line 127) and the SBOM step (line 135) audit, eliminating the drift where `.[all]` could resolve different versions than the lockfile pins. The pattern is idiomatic and consistent with the core/audit jobs (lines 73, 152 use the same --require-hashes lock install). requirements.lock is the full 4692-line lock generated from the all-extras set, so coverage is not narrowed. An explanatory comment cites #F-013. No regression.

Complete: Regression test tests/test_supply_chain.py::test_license_gate_scans_pinned_lockfile_not_live_resolution (lines 219-224) slices the gate section starting at "License gate" and asserts (a) `pip install -e ".[all]"` is absent and (b) `--require-hashes -r requirements.lock` is present. Both assertions discriminate the fix. Verified empirically: the test passes on the fixed tree, and when I spliced the old (1e3dce5) gate block back in to simulate a scoped revert of F-013, the test FAILED at line 223. Tree restored clean afterward. Both appropriate and complete.

content.text: Confirmed F-013. The license gate now scans the hash-pinned `requirements.lock` set (via `pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock` + `--no-deps -e .`) instead of a live `pip install -e ".[all]"` resolution, matching what pip-audit and the SBOM step audit. The regression test discriminates the fix — empirically verified to pass on the fixed tree and fail on a scoped revert.
